title: LNCP High-Intent Lexicon
description: Modal and epistemic markers for High-Intent word detection. These words signal how a writer positions their statements—their stance toward certainty, possibility, belief, and evidence.
note: In Peircean terms, these markers shape how interpretants form. They do not determine content meaning, but they modulate how meaning is held—tentatively, firmly, evidentially, or conditionally.
changelog: v0.5.0: Removed 'were' from CONDITIONALITY (false positive). Added stance detection categories.

CERTAINTY.description: Markers of strong commitment to truth or necessity. These words close down interpretive space.
CERTAINTY.interpretant_effect: Stabilizes meaning; signals the writer treats the claim as settled.

POSSIBILITY.description: Markers of openness, contingency, or uncertainty. These words expand interpretive space.
POSSIBILITY.interpretant_effect: Destabilizes meaning; signals the writer holds the claim provisionally.

BELIEF.description: Markers of subjective stance or cognitive state. These words foreground the writer's perspective.
BELIEF.interpretant_effect: Indexes the writer as source; meaning is anchored to a subject.

EVIDENCE.description: Markers of perceptual or inferential grounding. These words signal how the writer knows.
EVIDENCE.interpretant_effect: Grounds meaning in observation or inference; invites verification.

HEDGING.description: Markers of degree modulation or qualification. These words soften or temper claims.
HEDGING.interpretant_effect: Creates interpretive buffer; signals the writer is not fully committed to extent.

CONDITIONALITY.description: Markers of contingent or hypothetical framing. These words establish dependency relationships.
CONDITIONALITY.interpretant_effect: Suspends meaning; ties interpretation to unstated conditions.
CONDITIONALITY.note: Removed 'were' in v0.5.0 - too many false positives as past tense verb

CONTRAST.description: Markers of opposition, exception, or counter-expectation. These words create interpretive tension.
CONTRAST.interpretant_effect: Introduces polarity; signals the writer is navigating between positions.

EMPHASIS.description: Markers of intensification or focus. These words direct interpretive attention.
EMPHASIS.interpretant_effect: Amplifies salience; signals the writer wants this noticed.

multi_word_expressions.note: Some High-Intent markers are multi-word. These should be detected as units.

stance_detection.note: v0.5.0: New section for detecting stance patterns

OPEN.description: Predominantly opening markers; writer leaves interpretive space

CLOSED.description: Predominantly closing markers; writer asserts with confidence

CONTRADICTORY.description: Both opening AND closing markers present; mixed signals

BALANCED.description: Roughly equal opening and closing markers
BALANCED.condition: opening > 0 AND closing > 0 AND NOT contradictory

MINIMAL.description: Few or no epistemic markers detected

false_positive_exclusions.note: Words that appear in categories but should be excluded in certain contexts

rules.reason: Often ability ('you can do this') not possibility

profile_dimensions.description: How much interpretive space the writer leaves
How much the writer foregrounds their own perspective
How strongly the writer commits to their claims

usage_guidance.detection: Match words case-insensitively. Check multi-word expressions before single words. Record position and category for each match.
usage_guidance.interpretation: High-Intent markers do not judge content quality. They reveal how the writer holds their meaning—openly or firmly, personally or evidentially.
usage_guidance.presentation: When surfacing High-Intent patterns to users, remain non-directive. Describe what is present, not what should be.
usage_guidance.minimal_feedback: When few markers are detected, note the directness rather than suggesting something is missing.
